course_subtitle: The Open Compute Project began in 2011 with a simple but radical idea: openly sharing hardware designs to maximize innovation and reduce infrastructure complexity.
course_description_html:
The Open Compute Project began in 2011 with a simple but radical idea: openly sharing hardware designs to maximize innovation and reduce infrastructure complexity.

What started as a localized mission to build the most energy efficient data center has transformed into a massive global community shaping the future of scalable computing. In this module, you will explore the physical limitations that sparked this hardware revolution, understand the core tenets that guide the community, and discover how open infrastructure provides the vital blueprint for the high density AI Factories of tomorrow.

blocks.body_html:
Welcome to the Open Compute Project

In this module, you will explore the origins of the Open Compute Project (OCP) and analyze the critical engineering challenges that led to its formation. You will examine how a collaboration between Meta, Intel, and Rackspace transformed a proprietary industry into a $132B ecosystem of open collaboration as of 2025.

And it's growing rapidly!

blocks.heading_html: By the end of this module, you will be able to:

items_html: Analyze the "efficiency wall" that traditional data centers hit in 2011.
Identify the founding members and core mission of OCP.
Trace the evolution of the Open Rack standard from ORv1 to ORv3.
Contrast proprietary hardware models with the open innovation model required for modern AI Factories.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

media.alt: Building the Future of AI Hardware.mp4

blocks.caption_html: Building the future of AI hardware with OCP's  Dharmesh J. & Meta's Engineer Steve M.

blocks.body_html:
The data center landscape has shifted rapidly. As experts like Dharmesh from OCP and Steve M. from Meta highlight in the following discussion, we are no longer just building general purpose cloud data centers.

The industry is transitioning to massive, high density "AI Factories" designed to support unprecedented computational scale.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
Proprietary hardware simply couldn't keep up with the physical demands of this transition.

Click play to learn how the OCP open innovation model provides the crucial blueprint for this revolution.

blocks.quote_html: "We didn't just need a better server. We needed to rethink the data center from the grid to the gate."

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.heading_html: How the Revolution is Organized: Foundation vs. Community

blocks.body_html:
Breaking the efficiency wall required a massive collaborative effort.

To sustain this global innovation, OCP is structured into two distinct but deeply connected parts:

items.title: The OCP Foundation
items.description_html:
The non-profit entity that governs the organization. It provides the legal framework, manages intellectual property, and organizes global summits.

Remarkably, this massive global standard is supported by a lean Foundation staff of roughly 30 people.

items.title: The OCP Community
items.description_html: The actual engine of innovation. Consisting of tens of thousands of participants and engineers from hundreds of companies worldwide, this community actively debates, collaborates, and contributes the open hardware specifications that solve the industry's toughest challenges.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
Every contribution submitted by the Community must ensure it aligns with the guiding principles of the organization.

The following real world scenarios are mapped to the 5 core tenets of OCP.

items_html: Efficiency: Removing vanity faceplates from servers to improve airflow and reduce power draw.
Impact: Designing a modular chassis that solves a pervasive industry bottleneck and benefits the broader market.
Openness: Publishing complete mechanical CAD files so any manufacturer can build and verify the hardware.
Scale: Engineering a blind mate liquid cooling manifold that allows technicians to deploy hundreds of nodes seamlessly.
Sustainability: Maximizing the reuse of IT gear through circular economy principles while ensuring transparency of environmental impacts. We aspire to improve sustainability outcomes over time.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

items.title: The 19" Rack Bottleneck
items.description_html:
Before OCP, data centers relied on proprietary, disparate hardware.

"Gratuitous differentiation" led to inefficiency, blocked airflow, and trapped heat.

items.title: ORv1 (The Breakthrough)
items.description_html:
Open Rack version 1 originated as Facebook’s clean-sheet contribution to the Open Compute Project in early 2013, developed within the Rack & Power working group to replace inefficient legacy 19-inch EIA-310 racks with a wider, purpose-built design optimized for hyperscale data centers featuring triplet server configurations, a new Open Unit height, front cabling, and integrated 12V DC power delivery.

The specification evolved from an internal draft through community feedback, culminating in its official release on August 7, 2013, when OCP published the full 1.0 standard, enabling open adoption and laying the foundation for subsequent versions.

items.title: ORv2 (The Standard)
items.description_html:
Built in collaboration with Google, ORv2 improves on v1 primarily for higher power density, lower losses (optional 48V reduces I²R losses vs. 12V, enabling thinner conductors and ~30% better efficiency than legacy designs), space savings (no battery cabinets), cost reduction (simpler busbar/PSU design), and better scalability in space-constrained or high-density data centers.

Both maintain the same overall rack power and OCP interoperability, but v2’s integrated features and 48V support made it more future-proof, leading toward later ORv3 refinements.

items.title: ORv3 (The AI Era)
items.description_html: Designed for the 100kW future. ORv3 introduces a 48V blind-mate busbar and integrated liquid cooling manifolds to handle the extreme thermal density of modern AI workloads.

buttons.label: ORv3 Course Series
buttons.description_html:
Open to expand your knowledge?

Learn more about Open Rack (ORv3) in the course series on that topic, or on the opencompute.org website under the Rack & Power project.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html: Click on each tab to learn more.

back.description_html: OCP Accepted™ designs allow facilities to deploy pre-integrated racks ("Plug and Play") rather than assembling components on-site.
Open designs optimize airflow and power conversion, allowing OCP data centers to achieve near-perfect Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) compared to the industry average of 1.5.
The 2025 forecasted market impact of OCP hardware. It is no longer a niche for hyperscalers; it is the supply chain for the Global 2000.
Modular components allow for specific upgrades (e.g., swapping compute sleds but keeping the chassis), significantly reducing e-waste.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
OCP does not operate in a vacuum. To ensure that hardware and software standards align globally, OCP partners with other major industry consortiums.

Click on each tab to learn about a few examples.

items.title: JEDEC
items.description_html: OCP collaborates closely with JEDEC to define standards for memory components, ensuring next generation HBM and DDR technologies integrate perfectly into open hardware platforms.

items.title: The Linux Foundation
items.description_html: Open hardware requires open software. OCP works alongside the Linux Foundation to ensure firmware and operating systems can seamlessly manage high density infrastructure.

items.title: Regional Utility
items.description_html: From dedicated chapters in Europe to focused workgroups in Asia Pacific, the OCP community tailors global standards to meet local regulatory and power grid requirements.

buttons.label: OCP Alliances Page
buttons.description_html:
Collaboration drives Innovation

Learn more about our alliances by clicking the button. OCP also collaborates with many standards, organizations, and foundations without formal alliances.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
While we hope you eventually collaborate and contribute to the OCP Community, even as passive participant there's much to be gained. Various job roles will find different value.

Explore the cards to discover how each role can benefit from active participation.

back.description_html: You leverage OCP specifications as a proven, interoperable baseline to design scalable data centers without getting locked into a single vendor.
You download open CAD files and schematics to accelerate your product development,  focusing your time on unique innovations rather than reinventing the wheel.
You rely on standardized front aisle serviceability and unified power solutions to deploy and maintain hardware faster and safer.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html: Test your knowledge from this module. Select the best answer for each question, then submit.

blocks.question_html: Who founded the Open Compute Project in 2011?
blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! Facebook (now Meta), Intel, and Rackspace launched OCP to share open-source hardware for more efficient data centers.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite. Facebook (now Meta), Intel, and Rackspace launched OCP to share open-source hardware for more efficient data centers.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.question_html: Which architectural shift did ORv3 introduce to handle AI power loads?

choices.text_html: Individual Power Supply Units (PSUs) for every server

blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! ORv3 introduced a 48V Busbar with Liquid Cooling integration.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite. ORv3 introduced a 48V Busbar with Liquid Cooling integration.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

items_html: OCP was born from the need to break the "efficiency wall" of 2011.
ORv3 is the current rack standard, designed specifically to support high-performance AI and liquid cooling.

blocks.body_html: In Module 2, we will look at The Ecosystem. You will learn how the OCP Community is governed and how you can contribute to the projects that are building the future.

buttons.description_html:
